fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap is the anchor holding of this portfolio — a high-conviction, bottom-up stock picker with a 24-year history. The fund's alpha of 15.76% and Sharpe of 1.11 confirm that its performance is genuinely skill-driven, not beta-chasing. At a lower beta of 0.92, it captures most market upside while cushioning downside. The fund's 5-year CAGR of 20.3% is among the strongest in its category. This is Sherlock's thesis on active management: a fund with a proven, high-ability manager beats random index tracking over 20-year horizons.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Small caps are where Sherlock's greatest finds live. Nippon India Small Cap has delivered a 22.3% CAGR over 5 years — the best in its category — with a Sharpe of 1.02 that confirms risk-adjusted returns. A 12.15% alpha over Nifty 50 shows the manager is genuinely discovering winners, not just riding cycles. At ₹22,264 Cr AUM, it has enough depth to avoid closet indexing while maintaining a concentrated portfolio. SIP step up here is critical — small cap wealth creation is a multi-year compounding story.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Motilal Oswal Large & Mid Cap is a high-conviction, concentrated portfolio built on a 'buy the thing that has gone down the most' philosophy — a genuine contrarian thesis. The fund has delivered 26.9% CAGR over 3 years with a Sharpe of 1.26, the highest in its category. At 14.92% alpha and a 1-year return of 24.2%, this is a proven active manager in a high-conviction style. It complements ICICI Pru's more balanced approach in the large & mid cap space, providing a different risk-on philosophy.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Nippon India Mid Cap is a category leader — 23.6% CAGR over 5 years, 27.4% over 3 years, and a Sharpe of 1.11. The fund has a ₹8,085 Cr AUM and one of the longest track records in the mid-cap space, giving it the depth to hold quality businesses through cycles. With a 11.65% alpha, the manager is clearly identifying mid-cap compounders before the market recognises them. This is the mid-cap wealth-creation engine of the portfolio, complementary to the small-cap holding.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap is Sherlock Holmes' most important non-equity thesis in one fund. At a beta of 0.52 (extremely low), it drifts far from the Indian market cycle — the manager actively hedges Indian equity risk by holding global assets like US tech and US dollar denominated instruments. This is the genuine global diversification play, not a closet index fund. The fund has a 17.8% 5-year CAGR and a 12.13% alpha. Crucially, it falls in the equity-taxed slab (LTCG at 12.5% after 1 year), making it far more tax-efficient than a debt fund for a 30% slab investor. This is the portfolio's crash-resilience engine.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: A 30% slab investor's greatest enemy is tax drag. Aggressive Hybrid funds are equity-taxed (LTCG at 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25L held >1 year), making them far more tax-efficient than a pure debt fund. ICICI Pru Equity & Debt has a beta of 0.65 (very defensive), alpha of 10.12%, and a 19.3% 5-year CAGR — it is genuine active management. This fund serves a dual role: as a daughter's foreign university education corpus (10-year horizon) and as the portfolio's stability cushion. The 10% allocation is deliberate — enough to protect the education corpus without underweighting equity. SIP-only mode to avoid lumpsum ill-timing.

plan.setup_phase: Month 1: Deploy ₹30L lumpsum across all 5 equity funds (₹7.5L + ₹6L + ₹5L + ₹5L + ₹5L). Do not deploy the remaining ₹15L in the debt instrument — let it accrue in your savings account or sweep into a liquid fund for emergency access (you are already holding ₹1Cr in liquid/overnight instruments). Month 1 SIPs: Start all 5 SIPs at ₹12,000–₹30,000/month as per the plan. Month 3: Switch the remaining ₹15L from savings account into ICICI Pru Equity & Debt Fund (the Aggressive Hybrid holding) as a 20% step-up SIP lumpsum.
review_checkpoints: Year 3 — Review small-cap performance vs benchmark. Exit Nippon India Small Cap if 12-month rolling return <18% and redeploy into mid-cap (Nippon India Mid Cap).
Year 5 — Daughter's education corpus target: ₹2 Cr. If you are below this target, begin a dedicated liquid fund/lumpsum deployment plan in Year 6.
Year 7 — Portfolio rebalancing: Trim Motilal Oswal Large & Mid Cap if it has outperformed ICICI Pru Large & Mid Cap for 2 consecutive quarters. Reinvest trim proceeds into Nippon India Small Cap.
Year 10 — Daughter's education corpus: Full exit of ICICI Pru Equity & Debt Fund. Deploy all proceeds into a conservative arbitrage fund or bank FD to lock in gains for university fees.

Year 12 — Retirement corpus review: At ₹55, you should be at ₹5–6Cr. If below target, step-up SIPs by 15% annually. If above target, consider partial de-risking into ELSS (equity-taxed, 80C eligible) for tax efficiency.

Year 17 — Final retirement planning phase: Shift Parag Parikh Flexi Cap and ICICI Pru Equity & Debt into a conservative hybrid or Aggressive Hybrid fund. Begin a systematic withdrawal plan (SWP) for monthly income.
Year 20 — Retirement: If corpus is ₹8Cr+, you have met your goal. If above, consider step-up SWP strategy to manage tax brackets efficiently.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Review portfolio every quarter, not every calendar year. Rebalance if any single fund underperforms its benchmark for 12 consecutive months. Reduce equity by 5% every year you approach the daughter's education goal — move ₹50,000/month from small-cap SIP into Aggressive Hybrid. Rebalance aggressively if Nifty 50 falls 20% — buy additional units in small-cap and mid-cap funds. Do NOT rebalance based on market noise alone — stick to the 12-month underperformance trigger. Parag Parikh Flexi Cap is exempt from quarterly review — only exit if its global asset allocation drifts >40% from its stated 65-80% Indian stocks thesis.
plan.rationale: Sherlock Holmes is a 38-year-old independent consultant with a 20-year horizon, aggressive risk appetite, ₹30L existing corpus, and ₹1.2L/month to invest. His three goals are: (1) Maximise real returns above inflation (10%+ CAGR), (2) Retire at 55 with ₹8Cr corpus, and (3) Fund daughter's foreign university education in 10 years. The plan is built around five core theses: (a) High-conviction active management — all funds have 10+ years of history, 12%+ alpha, and genuine divergences from benchmark (tracking error >9% — funds are not closet indexers), (b) Asset allocation as a strategic thesis — 80% high-conviction blended large/mid/small cap, 15% flexi-cap global diversifier, 5% hybrid stability cushion, (c) Tax efficiency — 100% equity-taxed instruments (equity-taxed slab LTCG at 12.5% for 30% slab investors is superior to debt fund taxation at 7% slab), (d) SIP step-up at 12% annually (aligned with salary growth assumptions), and (e) A dedicated 10-year education corpus held in ICICI Pru Equity & Debt Fund (Aggressive Hybrid) with SIP-only mode to smooth volatility over the decade. The ₹30L lumpsum is deployed across the five equity funds (₹7.5L, ₹6L, ₹5L, ₹5L, ₹5L) — not the liquid/debt instrument, as the latter should be kept as liquidity buffer.
risks: Small-cap concentration (25% of portfolio in Nippon India Small Cap and Motilal Oswal Large & Mid Cap) carries higher volatility — expect 30-40% drawdowns in bad market cycles

No pure gold allocation — gold is not recommended for a 30% slab investor. A gold ETF or Sovereign Gold Bond (SGB) would be taxed at slab rates (30%), which is expensive compared to equity-taxed alternatives

10-year education corpus in Aggressive Hybrid fund — if the market crashes 40% in Year 6-7 (daughter's education timeline), the corpus could fall below ₹1.2Cr. Emergency liquidity should be separately maintained
Parag Parikh Flexi Cap's low beta (0.52) means it will underperform the Indian market in strong upcycles — this is deliberate strategy, not a flaw
All equity funds are actively managed — if any fund manager loses conviction or the fund house changes strategy, performance could decay. Quarterly review is essential
No ELSS allocation — ₹1.5L 80C is not utilised in this plan. If you are eligible, consider adding 3 ELSS units of ₹50,000 each to save ₹~50,000 annually in tax and still keep the equity-taxed benefit
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase total SIP by 12% every year to match salary growth. This alone can add ₹3-4 Cr to the corpus over 20 years. At ₹1.2L/month today, SIPs step up to ₹6.5L/month by year 15.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Start — aggressive equity, 15% in Aggressive Hybrid as education corpus
Year 10 — Daughter's education goal nears. Begin shifting ₹20,000/month from small-cap SIP to liquid fund. Partial exit of Aggressive Hybrid to lock education corpus.
Year 15 — Retirement 10 years away. Begin systematic de-risking: trim small-cap SIP, shift mid-cap gains into Aggressive Hybrid. Review education corpus at Year 11.
Year 20 — Retirement age 55 approached. Shift 50% of equity corpus into Aggressive Hybrid and conservative hybrid funds. Begin systematic withdrawal planning (SWP).
Year 25 — Post-retirement income phase. Move remaining equity to arbitrage funds. Gold ETFs added for inflation hedge.

plan.perspective: Sherlock Holmes is a thinker — this plan is built for someone who reads quarterly reports, tracks alphas and betas, and demands evidence. Every fund in this plan has a Sharpe ratio above 0.8, an alpha above 10%, and a tracking error above 9% (proof that it is genuinely active management, not closet indexing). No funds with less than 10 years of history are included. The portfolio is 85% equity, 15% hybrid — aggressive but with a defensive buffer. The education goal is protected in the Aggressive Hybrid fund, which is equity-taxed but far less volatile than a pure small-cap holding. The ₹30L lumpsum is deployed across the portfolio immediately — do not sit on it in a savings account.

aps.reasoning: This is an extremely active portfolio across all dimensions. Equity allocation is 100% actively managed funds with high alpha (10-15%), high tracking error (9-12%), and explicit focus on manager skill. Rebalancing guidelines call for quarterly reviews and tactical shifts based on performance triggers. Cost minimization is not emphasized - expense ratios range 1.0-1.2% and are secondary to alpha generation. The plan emphasizes deep fund-specific research with detailed analysis of manager track records and investment philosophies. While the time horizon is long-term (20 years), the portfolio construction is highly active with frequent monitoring and rebalancing. The portfolio activeness score reflects genuinely active management with concentrated bets and high tracking errors across all equity holdings.

pqs.reasoning: This is an exceptionally well-crafted plan for Sherlock Holmes' profile. Goal alignment is nearly perfect - the 20-year aggressive equity-heavy allocation directly targets his 8Cr retirement goal and 10-year education funding need. The plan acknowledges his data-driven nature with detailed fund analysis and clear rationales. Diversification is strong across large/mid/small caps and fund houses, though there's some concentration in Nippon India funds (2 holdings) and high equity exposure overall. Risk-return appropriateness is excellent for an aggressive investor - projected returns are realistic and risks are well-articulated. Internal consistency is very high - rationales, allocations, and risk warnings form a coherent story. Tax efficiency is good but not perfect - the plan correctly uses equity-taxed instruments for a 30% slab investor and mentions ELSS opportunity, but doesn't fully optimize 80C headroom and could benefit from more explicit tax optimization in the allocations.
